content.text: To establish high standards for teaching practice aligned with the Eduversal Teacher Appraisal System, promote reflective and evidence-based pedagogy through Professional Learning Communities, support continuous professional growth through structured induction and mentoring, integrate STEAM education and 21st-century pedagogies across the curriculum, and ensure all students receive high-quality, culturally responsive instruction that maximizes learning and wellbeing.

1. All Students Can Learn High expectations for every student, regardless of background, prior achievement, or learning differences. Growth mindset principles guide our approach, recognizing that intelligence and abilities develop through effort, strategy, and support. The teacher's role is to remove barriers, provide scaffolding, and celebrate progress. 2. Teaching is Both Art and Science Science provides evidence-based strategies rooted in research on how students learn and effective instructional methods. Art brings responsiveness to context, relationships, creativity, and cultural awareness. Effective teaching balances structure with flexibility, adapting proven strategies to meet the unique needs of each classroom. 3. Learning is Active and Constructive Students learn by doing, thinking, discussing, and creating, not just listening. Teachers act as facilitators of learning rather than transmitters of information. Inquiry, problem-solving, collaboration, and STEAM approaches are emphasized to develop critical thinking and innovation. 4. Relationships Matter Positive teacher-student relationships form the foundation for learning. Students take risks, persist, and engage when they feel safe, valued, and connected. Culturally responsive teaching honors students' identities, experiences, and the diverse Indonesian context of our schools. 5. Assessment Informs Instruction Teaching and assessment are integrated, not separate processes. Formative assessment drives real-time instructional adjustments. The feedback loop of Teach → Assess → Reflect → Adjust → Re-teach ensures continuous improvement in student learning outcomes. 6. Continuous Improvement Through Collaboration Teaching is a profession requiring lifelong learning. Reflective practice involves analyzing what works, why, and for whom. Collaboration with colleagues through PLCs, seeking feedback, participating in the Development of Teaching Proficiency (DTP) program, and trying new approaches drive professional growth.
content.title: Core Beliefs About Effective Teaching

content.text: The Eduversal Teacher Appraisal System is a comprehensive, evidence-based framework designed to elevate teaching quality, foster professional development, and improve student learning outcomes across all Eduversal Partner Schools. The system promotes transparency, accountability, and continuous improvement while recognizing and rewarding effective teaching.
content.title: Purpose

content.text: The annual appraisal cycle consists of five key phases: Phase 1: Orientation (Beginning of Academic Year) The Eduversal Appraisal Team conducts socialization programs for school management board members and teachers, introducing the appraisal timeline, rubrics, and expectations. Each school ensures all teachers receive orientation to the system and ongoing support in understanding the rubric's application. Phase 2: Data Collection Throughout the Year Schools collect evidence continuously through:

items: Administrative document reviews
Informal walkthrough observations by school leadership
Teacher's work inspection evidence (professional responsibilities)
Student satisfaction surveys (end of each semester)
content.title: 7.3.1 Appraisal Cycle and Timeline

content.text: Phase 3: Appraisal Visit (Typically Starts in Late August) The Eduversal Appraisal Team visits each partner school for comprehensive evaluation:

items: Dates: Typically 2-3 day visit each semester / academic year
Duration: 2 days per school
Activities: Subject appraisals, walkthrough observations, document reviews, post-observation conferences
Teacher Feedback Sessions: 30-45 minutes per observed teacher (same day or following day)
School Management Meeting: 60-90 minutes to review findings across all domains
Oral Exit Report: Presented on the last day of the visit
Written Report: Detailed appraisal report provided within 10 business days

content.text: Phase 5: Follow-Through and Professional Growth Planning

items: Final scores and predicates published in EduNav
Individual professional development recommendations
School-wide action planning for areas of growth
Integration with DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency) program for targeted support

content.text: The Eduversal Teacher Appraisal System evaluates teachers across four comprehensive domains:
content.title: 7.3.2 Four Appraisal Domains

content.text: Purpose: Assess the teacher's completion and timely submission of required administrative documents and professional responsibilities. Key Components:

items: Annual teaching plans and syllabus documents
Weekly and unit lesson plans
Student attendance records
Gradebooks and assessment records
Communication logs with parents
Professional development documentation
Punctuality and task completion
content.title: Domain 1: Administration (20% of Final Score)

items: Document submission records (collected before appraisal visit)
Verification during appraisal visit by Eduversal team
School management attestation of administrative performance

content.text: Purpose: Evaluate teaching practices, curriculum delivery, and student engagement through direct lesson observation. Domain 2 is subdivided into four critical sub-domains, each assessed during the 35-45 minute subject appraisal observation: Sub-Domain 2a: Planning and Organization Evaluates the teacher's ability to design purposeful, standards-aligned, and differentiated learning experiences. Key Indicators:

items: Demonstrates knowledge of content and pedagogy
Demonstrates knowledge of students (backgrounds, needs, prior learning)
Sets clear, measurable learning objectives aligned to Cambridge curriculum and Indonesian CP
Designs coherent instruction with logical lesson structure
Plans assessments aligned to objectives
Prepares differentiation strategies for diverse learners
content.title: Domain 2: Classroom Observation (60% of Final Score)

content.text: Sub-Domain 2b: Classroom Management Addresses the teacher's skill in creating an environment conducive to learning, including both physical and interpersonal aspects. Key Indicators:

items: Creates an environment of respect and rapport
Establishes a culture for learning (high expectations, normalizing mistakes, encouraging curiosity)
Manages classroom procedures effectively (routines, transitions, materials)
Manages student behavior proactively and respectfully
Organizes physical space to support learning

content.text: Sub-Domain 2c: Instructional Process Concerned with the teacher's skill in engaging students in learning through a wide range of effective instructional strategies. Key Indicators:

items: Communicates clearly and accurately
Uses varied questioning and discussion techniques
Engages students in active learning
Uses formative assessment during instruction
Demonstrates flexibility and responsiveness to student needs
Integrates STEAM methodologies where appropriate
Shows determination and persistence in supporting student learning

content.text: Sub-Domain 2d: Assessment Evaluates how the teacher uses assessment to measure and support student learning. Key Indicators:

items: Communicates clear assessment criteria and success standards to students
Uses formative assessment strategies throughout the lesson
Maintains accurate records of student performance
Provides timely and specific feedback to students
Uses assessment data to inform instructional decisions
Subjects Observed: English, Mathematics, Science, Biology, Chemistry, Physics, STEAM, and Religion
Duration: 35-45 minutes per subject appraisal
Selection: 6-8 lessons observed daily during appraisal visit (28-29 August), selected from school timetable
Requirement: All core subject teachers must be observed; temporary timetable adjustments made if necessary
Process: Appraiser attends full lesson, takes detailed notes using rubric, may interact minimally with students or teacher for clarification

content.text: Purpose: Gather evidence of professionalism, adherence to code of ethics, contributions to school community, and professional growth throughout the year. Key Components: Professional Responsibilities:

items: Reflection on teaching practice
Communication with families and stakeholders
Participation in professional development
Contributions to Professional Learning Communities (PLCs)
Adherence to school policies and professional standards
content.title: Domain 3: Teacher's Work Inspection (20% of Final Score)

items: Punctuality and reliability
Ethical conduct and confidentiality
Respectful interactions with all stakeholders
Professional appearance and demeanor
Responsiveness to feedback and continuous improvement efforts
School administrator assessments
PLC meeting attendance and participation records
Parent communication logs
Professional development certificates and reflections
Contributions to school events and extracurricular activities
Peer and leadership observations throughout the year

content.text: Scoring Method: Domain 3 is scored by school administrators based on cumulative evidence gathered throughout the year, not during the appraisal visit observation.
Purpose: Gather confidential student feedback on teaching effectiveness to identify strengths and areas for improvement. Survey Administration:

items: Frequency: End of each semester
Platform: Students complete surveys through their EduNav accounts
Confidentiality: Responses are anonymous to encourage honest feedback
Topics Covered:
Clarity of instruction and explanations
Teacher's ability to engage and motivate students
Fairness and consistency in assessment
Approachability and support for student learning
Classroom environment and management
Use of technology and varied teaching methods
content.title: Domain 4: Student Satisfaction Survey

items: Inform teacher self-reflection and professional growth goals
Identify trends in student perceptions across the school
Provide complementary evidence to observation data
Support coaching conversations and targeted professional development
Note: Survey data is developmental and not directly scored in final predicate calculation, but informs holistic performance review

content.text: Each indicator across all domains is evaluated using a four-level performance rubric: Note: Teachers are expected to demonstrate Proficient (Level 3) performance across all indicators as the baseline expectation for effective teaching. For detailed rubrics with specific indicators for each sub-domain, see Appendix 7-A: Teacher Appraisal Rubrics (Detailed Indicators).

Rating Calculation: Each indicator observed is rated 1-4 based on the rubric. Domain scores are calculated as follows: Final Score Calculation: Final Score \= (Domain 1 Score × 0.20) \+ (Domain 2 Score × 0.60) \+ (Domain 3 Score × 0.20) Domain scores are the average of all indicators within that domain, on a scale of 1-4, then converted to 0-100 scale for final scoring. Predicate Assignment: Target Standard: All teachers should achieve a minimum of Predicate B (Proficient) to demonstrate effective teaching aligned with Eduversal standards. Publication of Results:

items: Final scores and predicates published in EduNav within 2 weeks of appraisal visit
Individual teacher reports accessible to teacher, mentor, and school leadership
Aggregate school results shared with school management for strategic planning
content.title: 7.3.4 Scoring and Predicates

content.text: Subject Appraisals (Formal Observations) Purpose: Structured, formal observations designed to evaluate teaching practices, curriculum delivery, and student engagement in core subjects. Subjects Evaluated:

items: English
Mathematics
Science
Biology
Chemistry
Physics
STEAM (integrated Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, Mathematics)
Religion (Islamic Education or other as applicable)
content.title: 7.3.5 Subject Appraisals and Walkthrough Observations

steps: Pre-Observation: Appraisal team reviews teacher's lesson plan and student context information submitted by 25 August
Observation: 35-45 minute observation covering one full lesson or significant portion
Note-Taking: Appraiser uses printed or online observation form documenting evidence across all four sub-domains (Planning & Organization, Classroom Management, Instructional Process, Assessment)
Student Interaction: Appraiser may interact minimally with students or teacher to clarify context, ensuring minimal disruption
Focus: How teaching practices support student learning outcomes and reflect the school's commitment to academic excellence and Cambridge/Indonesian curriculum alignment

items: Appraisal Team Leader provides detailed observation schedule by 26 August
All core subject teachers included in schedule
Temporary timetable adjustments made if teachers not scheduled on visit days

content.text: Walkthrough Observations (Informal, Snapshot Visits) Purpose: Provide quick, low-stakes snapshots of classroom environment and teaching practices throughout the appraisal visit. Characteristics:

items: Duration: 5-10 minutes
Frequency: Approximately 4-6 walkthroughs per day during appraisal visit
Unannounced: No advance notice to teachers
Focus: General classroom culture, student engagement, use of space and resources, implementation of school-wide initiatives
Contribute to overall school appraisal (not individual teacher evaluations)
Inform school-level recommendations for professional development
Provide evidence for school appraisal domains (see School Appraisal Handbook for details)
May result in brief positive feedback to teachers but no formal scoring

content.text: Teacher Response: Teachers should deliver lessons as planned, reflecting usual classroom practice. Walkthroughs are supportive and non-evaluative for individual appraisal purposes.

Purpose: Establish shared understanding of lesson context, objectives, and anticipated student responses before the formal observation. Participants: Teacher and appraiser (HOD, Academic Coordinator, Principal, or Eduversal appraisal team member) Timing: 1-2 days before scheduled observation (15-20 minutes) Discussion Topics:

items: Lesson objectives and alignment to curriculum standards (Cambridge and/or Indonesian CP)
Student context: Where students are in the unit, prior learning, any particular challenges or needs
Instructional strategies planned and rationale
Anticipated student responses and how teacher will address misconceptions
Assessment methods to be used during lesson
Differentiation strategies for diverse learners (EAL, SEN, advanced)
Teacher's focus area for feedback (e.g., "I want specific feedback on my questioning techniques to promote higher-order thinking")
content.title: 7.4.1 Pre-Observation Conference

items: Lesson plan
Unit overview (optional)
Student work samples or assessment data (optional, to provide context)
Ask clarifying questions to understand teacher's planning and decision-making
Discuss observation focus agreed with teacher
Explain observation process and rubric
Create supportive, collaborative tone (appraisal is for growth, not "gotcha")
Observer arrives quietly and sits in unobtrusive location
Minimal disruption to lesson flow
Observer may have printed observation form or digital device for notes
content.title: 7.4.2 Lesson Observation

items: Evidence Collection: Observer takes detailed, objective notes on what teacher and students are doing and saying
Focus Areas: Planning & Organization, Classroom Management, Instructional Process, Assessment (Domain 2 sub-domains)
Rubric Use: Observer references rubric indicators while observing, noting examples of each performance level
Student Interaction (Optional): Observer may ask students what they're learning, look at student work, or ask teacher brief clarifying questions—always respectfully and minimizing disruption
Look for evidence, not judgment: "Teacher used think-pair-share to engage all students" (evidence) vs. "Teacher is good at engagement" (judgment)
Notice patterns: One incident doesn't define performance; look for consistent practices
Consider context: EAL learners, class size, time of day, resources available
Interrupt lesson to correct teacher or redirect students
Engage in extended conversations during instruction
Evaluate based on personal preferences rather than rubric criteria
Make snap judgments without sufficient evidence

content.text: Purpose: Provide quality, evidence-based feedback to guide professional growth over time. Timing: Within 3 days of observation (ideally same day or next day) (30-45 minutes) Structure: 1. Teacher Reflection (5-10 minutes) Teacher shares self-assessment first:

items: What went well in the lesson?
What would you change if you taught this lesson again?
How did students respond? Did they meet the objectives?
Any surprises or challenges?
content.title: 7.4.3 Post-Observation Conference

content.text: Rationale: Starting with teacher reflection promotes ownership of professional growth and helps observer understand teacher's perspective. 2. Observer Feedback (15-20 minutes) Commendations (Start with Strengths)

items: Specific examples of effective practice observed
Tied to rubric indicators

Example: "Your use of wait time after posing questions allowed all students time to formulate responses. I observed at least 5 seconds of silence after each question, which is a research-based best practice for promoting deeper thinking. This aligns with Distinguished performance in the 'Uses Questioning and Discussion Techniques' indicator."
1-2 specific, actionable suggestions (avoid overwhelming with too many)
Tied to rubric indicators
Evidence-based, not opinion-based

Example: "To increase rigor and higher-order thinking, consider asking more 'why' and 'how' questions rather than primarily 'what' questions. During the lesson, I noted approximately 80% of questions were recall-level. Incorporating analysis and evaluation questions would move practice toward Proficient/Distinguished in the Questioning indicator."
Use "I noticed..." language rather than "You should..."
Reference specific moments from the lesson
Avoid educational jargon; be clear and concrete
Balance praise and growth areas authentically (don't manufacture false praise)
Collaboratively set 1-2 professional growth goals for next observation or term
Goals should be SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound)

Example Goal: "By the next observation in January, I will incorporate at least 3 higher-order thinking questions (analyze, evaluate, create levels of Bloom's Taxonomy) in each lesson, and use wait time of at least 5 seconds after each question."
Teacher commits to trying specific strategies
Observer provides resources or support:
Professional development opportunities (DTP webinars, Cambridge PD, peer observation)
Instructional resources (lesson plan examples, articles, videos)
Coaching or mentoring if needed
Written observation report (commendations, recommendations, goals) shared with teacher within 1 week
Teacher signs to acknowledge receipt (signature ≠ agreement, just confirms they received the report)
Report stored in teacher's professional file in EduNav
Teacher may add written response if desired
Supportive and collaborative, not punitive
Recognize teaching is complex; perfection is not the expectation
Growth mindset: "What you're working on" not "what you're bad at"
Celebrate progress and effort

content.text: When Used: When a teacher is consistently rated Basic (Level 2) or Unsatisfactory (Level 1) on observations, or when a new teacher needs intensive support. Purpose: Provide targeted, structured support to help teachers improve specific areas of practice through a coaching cycle. Coaching Cycle Process (6-12 weeks): Step 1: Goal Setting Meeting

items: Identify 1-2 specific, measurable goals based on observation data
Example: "Increase student participation from 40% to 70% of class by end of term"
Root cause analysis: Why is this area challenging? (skill gap, knowledge gap, resources, student behavior, other factors)
Define success criteria: What will improvement look like?
content.title: 7.4.4 Coaching Cycle for Improvement

items: Coach (HOD, Academic Coordinator, or experienced peer) and teacher plan lessons together
Coach models strategies, provides resources, discusses anticipated challenges
Teacher practices explaining new strategy to build confidence
Option A: Coach teaches lesson while teacher observes (modeling)
Teacher takes notes on specific strategies coach uses
Debrief immediately after: What did you notice? What questions do you have?
Option B: Coach and teacher co-teach (gradual release)
Coach takes lead on challenging part; teacher observes and assists
Next lesson, roles reverse with teacher taking lead and coach supporting
Coach observes teacher implementing strategy in their own class
Quick debrief right after lesson (5-10 minutes):
What went well? What was challenging?
What will you adjust next time?
Feedback is formative, focused on growth (not evaluative for appraisal)
Teacher continues practicing strategy independently in subsequent lessons
Reflects in journal or through structured reflection prompts
May video-record self (optional) to self-assess
Formal observation to assess progress toward goal (after 6-12 weeks of coaching)
Celebrate growth and improvement
Set next goal if needed, or transition back to standard observation cycle if proficient
Weekly check-ins (15-30 minutes) during active coaching phase
Monthly observations during coaching cycle
More intensive support (daily or every-other-day) for teachers in urgent need
Coaching log with dates, focus areas, strategies tried, and progress notes
Stored confidentially; used for support planning, not punitive evaluation

content.text: Mindset: Coaching is supportive, not punitive. The goal is to help every teacher reach Proficient performance. Struggling does not mean failing; it means needing more targeted support. For intensive support needs, teachers may also be referred to DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency) program. See Section 9.4 for DTP details.
Purpose: Structured support for teachers rated Basic (Level 2) or Unsatisfactory (Level 1) for two consecutive appraisals. PIP Process:

items: Trigger: Two consecutive Basic ratings OR one Unsatisfactory rating
Duration: One full term (10-12 weeks)
Components:
Written improvement plan with SMART goals
Weekly coaching sessions with HOD/Academic Coordinator
Bi-weekly formal observations with written feedback
Access to targeted PD (DTP workshops, peer mentoring)
Mid-term review checkpoint
Outcomes:
Improved to Proficient → Exit PIP, continue normal appraisal cycle
Still Basic → Extend PIP one more term
Still Unsatisfactory → Recommend contract non-renewal (per Indonesian labor law)
Documentation: All PIP records stored in EduNav and teacher's personnel file.
content.title: 7.4.5 Performance Improvement Plans (PIPs)

content.text: See Principal's Handbook Section [X] for full Capability Procedure and HR implications.
Purpose: Develop self-awareness by analyzing one's own teaching. Process:

items: Teacher records 15-20 min segment of their lesson (focus on specific skill)
Watch video alone or with mentor, using observation rubric
Identify: 1 strength, 1 area for growth, 1 concrete action to try next time
Optional: Share clips in PLC for collaborative analysis
content.title: 7.4.6 Video Self-Reflection

content.text: Privacy: Videos stored securely; used only for teacher's own development (not for appraisal).
When Feedback is Met with Resistance:

steps: Listen First: "Tell me more about your perspective."
Use Evidence: "I observed X happening. What were you trying to achieve?"
Avoid Judgment: "This strategy didn't engage students" (not "You're a bad teacher")
Focus on Impact: "When you called on only 3 students, 25 others weren't engaged."
Collaborative Problem-Solving: "What might we try differently next time?"
content.title: 7.4.7 Navigating Difficult Feedback Conversations

content.text: When to Escalate: If teacher refuses feedback or shows no improvement after 2 coaching cycles, involve Principal and initiate PIP (see 7.4.5).

Provide structured, comprehensive support to new teachers (those in their first or second year at an Eduversal Partner School) to ensure successful integration into the school community, understanding of school culture and expectations, and development of effective teaching practices from day one.
content.title: Purpose

content.text: Through mentorship, peer collaboration, and ongoing professional development, the New Teacher Induction Program aims to:

items: Provide guidance needed to master the complexities of modern education in a Cambridge/Indonesian dual curriculum context
Empower new teachers to deliver high-quality education from the start
Foster a sense of belonging and commitment among new staff members
Build a strong foundation for long-term teacher retention and job satisfaction
content.title: Program Philosophy

content.text: The New Teacher Induction Program is a year-long process divided into three main stages, preceded by a preparation phase:
content.title: 7.5.1 Three-Stage Induction Model

content.text: Led by: School management and administrative staff Purpose: Complete all logistical and preparatory tasks before new teacher arrival Activities:

items: Welcome Pack Preparation: School-branded items, staff list, school map, academic calendar, key policies summary
Account Setup: Email, EduNav access, online learning platforms (Google Classroom, etc.), physical access cards/keys
Mentor Assignment: School leader selects mentor (typically subject leader or experienced teacher in same department) and briefs mentor on responsibilities
Classroom Setup: Assign classroom, ensure furniture, technology, and basic supplies ready
Onboarding Schedule: Finalize schedule for first week orientation activities
content.title: Stage 0: Meeting and Preparation (June)

content.text: Purpose: Orientation activities to help new teacher settle and understand school environment Duration: 3 days (can be adjusted based on school schedule) Day 1: Welcome and Introduction

items: Meet key staff: Principal, Academic Coordinator, HOD, mentor, counselor, administrative staff
Overview of school culture, mission, vision, and core values (Eduversal philosophy, SLOs, Profil Pelajar Pancasila)
Introduction to core policies: curriculum, behavior management, communication procedures, safeguarding
Complete administrative tasks: employment forms, tax documents, bank details, emergency contacts
School tour: classrooms, staff room, library, science labs, STEAM makerspace (if applicable), prayer rooms, canteen, boarding facilities (if applicable), admin offices
Receive welcome pack and keys/access cards
content.title: Stage 1: Familiarization and Welcoming (First Week of July)

items: Curriculum Overview: Cambridge curriculum expectations (schemes of work, assessment objectives), Indonesian national subject requirements, SLO integration
Resources and Materials: Introduction to available teaching resources, textbooks, digital platforms, STEAM equipment, library services
Technology Training: School LMS (Google Classroom, EduNav), projector/smartboard use, printing/copying procedures, student devices policy
Lesson Planning Guidance: School expectations for lesson plans, where to submit, sample lesson plans reviewed (see Section 7.6)
Assessment Practices: Overview of formative and summative assessment expectations, grading policies, reporting timelines
Professional Development Opportunities: Introduction to DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency) program, Cambridge PD courses, in-school PD calendar
Mentoring Program: Formal introduction to assigned mentor, explanation of mentor-mentee meeting schedule and expectations
Code of Conduct and Professionalism: Dress code, punctuality, communication standards, social media guidelines, ethical responsibilities
Teacher Evaluation Process: Overview of Eduversal Teacher Appraisal System (Section 7.3), walkthrough observations, coaching support available
Safeguarding Training: Child protection policies, reporting procedures, boundaries (See Section 3)

content.text: Purpose: Hands-on preparation for the school year with mentor support Duration: First 2-3 months of academic year Mentee-Mentor Collaboration: New teacher and mentor meet regularly (weekly recommended) to:

items: Review and finalize class lists, student profiles (learning needs, EAL levels, SEN accommodations)
Prepare detailed lesson plans for first few weeks of term
Set up attendance records and gradebooks in EduNav
Gather and organize textbooks and supplementary materials
Plan Parent Orientation/Communication: Draft welcome letter to parents, prepare for Back-to-School Night, discuss parent communication strategies
Classroom Setup: Arrange furniture, create bulletin boards, establish classroom routines and rules
Review Teacher Evaluation Procedures: Go over appraisal rubric in detail, discuss what observers look for, prepare for first informal observation
Classroom Management Strategies: Discuss proactive behavior management, school discipline policies, culturally responsive approaches (Section 7.13)
Observe Mentor's Lessons: New teacher observes mentor teaching (at least 2-3 lessons in first month) to see effective practices modeled
content.title: Stage 2: Laying the Foundation (July-September)

items: Establish routines and build relationships with students
Implement planned lessons with mentor feedback
Begin informal observations by mentor (non-evaluative, for support)

content.text: Purpose: Continuous improvement through reflection, data analysis, and ongoing professional learning Duration: Remainder of first year Ongoing Reflection and Support:

items: Monthly Mentor-Mentee Meetings: Continue regular check-ins to discuss challenges, celebrate successes, analyze student data, plan upcoming units
Professional Development: New teacher engages in DTP webinars/workshops, attends PLCs, participates in school-based PD
Classroom Visits: New teachercontinues observing experienced teachers monthly to learn diverse strategies
Student Assessment Analysis: Review formative and summative assessment results with mentor; identify trends, gaps, and action steps for improvement
Mid-Year Check-In (January): School leader meets with new teacher to review progress, discuss any concerns, provide encouragement and support
Peer Collaboration: Participate actively in PLCs, contribute to collaborative planning, share emerging successes
content.title: Stage 3: Anchoring Professional Growth (October-May)

items: Monthly Observations by Mentor (Months 2-9): Mentor observes new teacher's lesson at least once per month using teacher appraisal form
Purpose: Developmental feedback, not punitive
Focus areas rotate based on new teacher's growth goals
Immediate post-observation debrief to discuss strengths and next steps
Formal Observation by School Leader (Q4, April-May):
School Principal or Academic Coordinator conducts formal lesson observation
Purpose: Assess competencies in planning, instruction, assessment, professionalism
Full observation cycle: pre-conference, observation, post-conference with written feedback
If weaknesses identified, provide specific feedback and additional support before end of year
Review Year's Growth: New teacher and mentor reflect together on progress across the year, celebrate achievements, identify areas for continued focus in Year 2
Self-Assessment: New teacher completes self-assessment using appraisal rubric
Plan for Year 2: Set professional goals for second year, discuss any changes in assignment or responsibilities
Close-Out Procedures: Final grades submitted, classroom packed up, end-of-year events participation, summer PD opportunities discussed

content.text: Mentor Selection: School leaders assign mentors to new teachers at the start of the induction program. Ideal mentors are:

items: Experienced teachers (typically 3+ years at the school)
Strong practitioners who model effective teaching (Proficient or Distinguished appraisal ratings)
Subject leaders (HODs) or teachers in the same department when possible
Skilled in providing constructive feedback
Committed to supporting new teacher growth
content.title: 7.5.2 Mentor-Mentee Partnership

items: Direct, Day-to-Day Support: Provide guidance in subject area, answer questions, share resources
Regular Meetings: Meet with mentee regularly (weekly recommended in first term, bi-weekly thereafter) to discuss established checklist items and emerging needs
Lesson Planning Guidance: Review mentee's lesson plans, provide feedback on alignment to curriculum and differentiation strategies
Observation Opportunities: Invite mentee to observe mentor's lessons; observe mentee's lessons monthly with developmental feedback
Problem-Solving Support: Help mentee navigate challenges (classroom management, parent communication, workload)
Advocate: Connect mentee to resources, professional development, and school leadership when additional support needed
Guide in Additional Responsibilities: Support mentee in extracurricular duties, homeroom responsibilities, school events participation

content.text: See [Cambridge Mentoring Guide] for detailed mentoring strategies. Mentee (New Teacher) Responsibilities:

items: Take Ownership: Actively engage with support and resources provided; recognize that professional growth is primarily self-driven
Be Proactive: Reach out to mentor with questions, request observations, seek feedback
Implement Feedback: Try new strategies suggested by mentor and observers; reflect on impact
Participate Fully: Attend all orientation sessions, PD opportunities, PLC meetings, school events
Communicate Openly: Share challenges honestly so mentor and school can provide appropriate support
Reflect Regularly: Keep a teaching journal or use structured reflection prompts to analyze practice
Standard: One full academic year for new teachers
Extendable: May be extended into second year based on teacher needs and school leader recommendation
Ongoing Informal Support: Even after formal mentoring ends, collegial relationships and PLCs continue to provide professional support
Months 1 (July): No formal observation; focus on settling in, building routines, establishing relationships
Months 2-9 (August-April): Mentor observes new teacher's lesson at least once per month
Month 10 (May): School leader conducts formal evaluation observation
content.title: 7.5.3 Monthly Observations and Support

content.text: Monthly Observation Process: Purpose: Developmental, formative feedback to support growth (not summative evaluation) Process:

steps: Brief Pre-Observation Discussion (Optional): Mentor and new teacher discuss lesson to be observed, identify focus area for feedback
Observation (30-45 minutes): Mentor observes full lesson, takes notes using simplified observation form based on appraisal rubric
Immediate Debrief (10-15 minutes): Right after lesson, mentor and new teacher discuss:

items: What went well?
What was challenging? Why?
What would you do differently next time?
Mentor shares 1-2 specific commendations and 1-2 actionable recommendations

steps: Documentation: Brief observation notes logged (date, focus area, key feedback) in mentoring file; used to track growth over time

content.text: Focus Areas Rotate Monthly: New teachers cannot improve everything at once. Focus on one domain or indicator at a time:

items: Month 2 (August): Classroom management and routines
Month 3 (September): Clear learning objectives and success criteria
Month 4 (October): Questioning techniques and student engagement
Month 5 (November): Formative assessment and feedback to students
Month 6 (December): Differentiation for diverse learners
Month 7 (January): Instructional pacing and time management
Month 8 (February): Assessment practices and record-keeping
Month 9 (March): Building on strengths; addressing persistent challenges
Month 10 (April-May): Comprehensive formal evaluation by school leader
Celebrate progress, not perfection
Recognize that teaching is complex and mastery takes time (3-5 years to become highly proficient)
Normalize struggle: All teachers, even experienced ones, face challenges
Frame feedback as "what you're working on" not "what you're doing wrong"

content.text: Realistic Expectations for New Teachers: New teachers are not expected to perform at Distinguished level. Realistic first-year goals:

items: By End of Year 1: Demonstrate Basic to Proficient (Level 2-3) performance across most indicators
Strengths in Some Areas: May show Proficient or even Distinguished practice in 2-3 areas (often Planning, Content Knowledge, or areas of personal strength)
Growth Areas Identified: Will have 2-4 indicators where performance is still Basic or Developing, with clear plan for improvement in Year 2
content.title: 7.5.4 First-Year Expectations

items: Classroom Management: Establishing authority, consistency with rules, managing transitions smoothly
Pacing: Lessons run too long or too short; difficulty adjusting in real-time
Differentiation: Meeting diverse learner needs simultaneously (EAL, SEN, advanced) is very challenging
Workload Management: Balancing planning, grading, meetings, extracurriculars; time management
Parent Communication: Navigating difficult conversations, cultural differences, language barriers (Indonesian/English)
Curriculum Depth: Still building deep content knowledge and pedagogical content knowledge
Reduced Responsibilities: Some schools reduce new teacher load (fewer preps, fewer extracurriculars in first semester)
Peer Observation Opportunities: Encouraged to observe 1-2 experienced teachers per month to learn strategies
Protected Planning Time: Mentors advocate for new teachers to have adequate planning time
Emotional Support: Check-ins on wellbeing, stress management resources, encouraging school culture
Differentiated PD: DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency) webinars designed for new teachers; foundational topics prioritized

content.text: Key Success Factors for New Teachers:

items: Ask for Help Early: Don't wait until struggles become crises; reach out to mentor or school leadership proactively
Build Relationships: With students, colleagues, parents—relationships are foundation of effective teaching
Stay Organized: Systems for lesson planning, grading, communication prevent overwhelm
Reflect Regularly: What worked? What didn't? Why? Use evidence (student work, observations) to inform changes
Take Care of Yourself: Teaching is demanding; prioritize sleep, healthy eating, exercise, hobbies to prevent burnout (See Section 7.14)
Embrace Growth Mindset: Mistakes are learning opportunities; teaching improves with deliberate practice over time

content.text: Ensure teachers thoughtfully plan instruction that maximizes student learning, aligns with curriculum standards, incorporates differentiation, and uses assessment to inform teaching.
content.title: Purpose

content.text: Before beginning new unit, teachers should assess students' prior knowledge and prerequisite skills:

steps: Review previous unit results
Conduct pre-assessment or entry-level diagnostic
Identify gaps to address during instruction
content.title: 7.6.0. Diagnostic Assessment (Before Teaching)

content.text: Diagnostic results inform differentiation strategies. See Assessment Policy Section 2.1 for diagnostic assessment framework.
Required Elements (for all lessons): 1. Header/Basic Information

items: Subject and grade level
Date and lesson number/unit title
Teacher name
Duration (e.g., 60 minutes)
content.title: 7.6.1 Lesson Plan Components

items: What students will know and be able to do by end of the lesson
Aligned to Cambridge curriculum learning objectives and/or Indonesian Capaian Pembelajaran (CP)
Clear, measurable, student-friendly language
Posted visibly in classroom at start of lesson: "By the end of today, you will be able to..."
"By the end of this lesson, you will be able to describe the process of photosynthesis and explain why plants need sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide."
How students (and teacher) will know objectives have been achieved
Can be written as "I can..." statements or rubric indicators
Helps students self-assess their progress
"I can label a diagram showing the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis."
"I can explain in my own words why plants need sunlight."
"I can write the word equation for photosynthesis correctly."
Formative Assessment: How will you check understanding during the lesson?
Methods: Exit tickets, whiteboard checks, questioning, observation, peer assessment, think-pair-share
Identify at least 2-3 formative assessment strategies per lesson
Summative Assessment (if applicable):
For end-of-unit or milestone lessons, indicate summative assessment (quiz, test, project, presentation)
Formative: Thumbs up/down check after explaining each step of photosynthesis; exit ticket asking students to draw and label photosynthesis diagram
Summative: End-of-unit test on plant processes (scheduled for next week)
List all materials needed: textbook pages, handouts, manipulatives, technology (projector, laptops, apps), lab equipment
Include links to videos, websites, digital files
Note any materials to be prepared in advance (photocopies, lab setup)
Textbook: Cambridge Lower Secondary Science, pages 45-48
Video: "Photosynthesis Explained" (YouTube, 4 min) \- [link]
Materials: Colored pencils, printed plant diagrams (1 per student), chart paper for group work

content.text: 6. Lesson Sequence Break lesson into logical phases with time allocations: Opening (5-10 minutes)

items: Hook/Engage: Capture student interest and activate prior knowledge
Examples: Intriguing question, short video, demonstration, real-world connection, review game
Review Prior Learning: Connect to previous lesson; check prerequisite knowledge
State Objectives and Success Criteria: Share learning goals clearly; ensure students know what they're working toward
Hook (3 min): Show time-lapse video of plant growing from seed. Ask: "Where does a plant get the energy and materials to grow this big?"
Prior Knowledge Review (2 min): Quick recap of last lesson on plant cell structure. "What organelle is found in plant cells but not animal cells? Why is it important?"
Share Objectives (2 min): Write objectives on board; students copy into notebooks. Clarify success criteria.
Teacher Explains, Demonstrates, Models new content
Use visuals, examples, think-aloud to make thinking visible
Check for understanding frequently: "Does everyone follow?" (thumbs up/down, quick questions)
For EAL students: Speak clearly, moderate pace, use visuals and gestures, pre-teach key vocabulary

Direct Instruction (8 min): Use diagram on board to explain photosynthesis process step-by-step. Model how to label diagram. Think-aloud: "I know chlorophyll is important, so I'll label the chloroplast first."
Check for Understanding (2 min): Quick whiteboard check \- students draw simple diagram showing sunlight going into leaf. Scan room to see who needs re-teaching.
Students Practice with Teacher Support
Work in pairs or small groups on structured task
Teacher circulates, provides feedback, addresses misconceptions, asks probing questions
Scaffold as needed; gradually release responsibility to students

Pair Work (12 min): Students work with partner to complete partially filled-in photosynthesis diagram (word bank provided). Teacher circulates, asks: "Why do you think the arrow points from sunlight to the leaf? What does that show?"
Address Misconceptions (3 min): If multiple students confused about role of carbon dioxide, briefly re-explain with new example.
Students Work Independently to apply learning
Teacher monitors, provides individual support as needed, takes notes on who's mastering vs. struggling (informs future instruction)

Independent Task (12 min): Students complete exit ticket \- draw and label photosynthesis diagram from memory, write 2-3 sentences explaining why plants need sunlight. Use colored pencils to color-code inputs/outputs.
Summarize Key Points: Review what was learned
Check for Understanding: Exit ticket, reflection question, quick quiz, self-assessment against success criteria
Preview Next Lesson: Build anticipation; connect to upcoming learning
Summary (2 min): "Today we learned that photosynthesis is how plants make food using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide. They produce glucose and oxygen."
Exit Ticket (collected)
Preview (1 min): "Tomorrow we'll explore why photosynthesis is important for all life on Earth, including us!"

content.text: 7. Differentiation How will you support diverse learners?

items: Struggling Students: Scaffolding strategies (graphic organizers, sentence frames, modified tasks, small group re-teaching, peer support, extended time)
Advanced Students: Extension activities (deeper questions, independent research, enrichment projects, leadership roles like peer tutoring)
EAL Students: Language support strategies (vocabulary pre-teaching, visuals, simplified language, use of L1 when appropriate, sentence frames)
SEN Students: Accommodations from IEP (preferential seating, assistive technology, reduced quantity, alternative formats)
Struggling: Provide word bank for diagram labeling; offer partially completed diagram as scaffold; pair with strong peer
Advanced: Challenge to research photosynthesis in extreme environments (desert, underwater plants); explain how adaptations work
EAL: Pre-teach vocabulary (photosynthesis, chlorophyll, glucose, oxygen) with visuals and gestures; provide diagram template with simplified language
SEN: Student with dyslexia receives digital copy of notes to use text-to-speech; extended time for exit ticket
What went well? (Be specific: which activities, strategies, moments?)
What didn't work as planned? Why? (Student confusion, timing issues, behavior problems, unclear instructions?)
What will you do differently next time? (Concrete changes)
Student Learning Evidence: Did students meet objectives? How do you know? (Use formative assessment data)
Example: "12 out of 18 students correctly labeled all parts of photosynthesis diagram on exit ticket. 6 students still confused about role of chlorophyll—will re-teach in small group tomorrow."
Identify patterns in teaching practice over time
Use evidence to improve future lessons (not just gut feelings)
Set professional growth goals based on reflection
Document progress for appraisal and professional development
Teachers may use school-provided template or their own format, as long as all required elements (Section 7.6.1) are included
Digital or handwritten acceptable (digital preferred for easy sharing and archiving)
Subject-specific templates available for specialized lessons:
Science lab lesson plan template
English workshop lesson plan template (reading/writing)
Math problem-solving lesson plan template
STEAM project-based learning plan template
content.title: 7.6.2 Lesson Plan Formats and Submission

items: Frequency: Determined by each school (typically weekly or by unit)
Common: Lesson plans for the following week submitted to HOD or Academic Coordinator by Friday for the next week
Some schools require daily lesson plans; others unit plans with daily breakdowns
Where to Submit:
Upload to shared drive (Google Drive folder, school server)
Submit via EduNav (if school uses this feature)
Email to HOD/Academic Coordinator
Physical copies (less common)
Review Process:
HOD or Academic Coordinator reviews plans to ensure alignment with curriculum, differentiation, assessment strategies
Feedback provided if significant revisions needed
Plans subject to review during observations and appraisal visits
Teachers maintain organized files of lesson plans (digital or physical)
Accessible during observations, appraisal visits, or if teacher absent (substitute needs plans)
Updated regularly; revisions based on reflection incorporated

content.text: Consult School Leadership: Each Eduversal Partner School may have specific expectations for lesson plan submission frequency, format, and review process. New teachers should clarify these expectations with their mentor or HOD during induction. Lesson Plan Template Available: See Appendix 7-B: Lesson Plan Template for school-provided format. 7.6.3 Subject-Specific Lesson Planning Considerations Mathematics:

items: Problem-solving focus: Use Polya's 4-step method
Concrete-Pictorial-Abstract (CPA) approach for conceptual understanding
Error analysis: Students analyze common mistakes
Inquiry-based learning: Pose question → Investigate → Explain → Apply
Safety protocols for lab work (always reviewed at lesson start)
Linking to real-world applications and local context
Reading: Pre-reading (activate prior knowledge) → During (annotation) → Post (discussion, writing)
Writing: Model → Guided practice → Independent application
Grammar in context (not isolated drills)

content.text: Teachers must conduct assessments according to minimum frequencies outlined in the Assessment Policy:

steps: Weekly: At least 2-3 formative checks per subject
Monthly: At least 1 recorded assessment per subject
Semester: Mid-term and final summative assessments
content.title: 7.6.3 Assessment Frequency Requirements:

content.text: See Assessment Policy Section 4.9 for complete requirements by key stage.
All formative and summative assessments must be recorded in the EduNav Gradebook within timelines specified in the Assessment Policy:

steps: Formative assessments: Ongoing, updated weekly
Summative assessments: Within 5 working days of completion
content.title: 7.6.4 Recording Assessments in EduNav:

content.text: Teachers must ensure gradebook categories align with school weighting structure. See Assessment Policy Section 7 for complete EduNav protocols.

Create collaborative teams of teachers working together to improve student learning through inquiry, data analysis, shared practice, and collective problem-solving. PLCs are the primary vehicle for job-embedded professional development in Eduversal Partner Schools.
content.title: Purpose

content.text: PLC Composition: PLCs can be organized in multiple ways depending on school size, structure, and focus:

items: Subject-Based PLCs: Teachers of the same subject across grades (all Math teachers, all Science teachers, all English teachers)
Best for: Curriculum alignment, vertical articulation, subject-specific pedagogy, exam preparation strategies
Grade-Level PLCs: Teachers of the same grade across subjects (all Grade 8 teachers)
Best for: Student-centered discussions, cross-curricular integration, consistent behavior management, grade-level initiatives
Vertical PLCs: Teachers across grade levels for the same subject (Grades 7-9 Math teachers, or Grades 10-12 Science teachers)
Best for: Ensuring progression of skills, alignment with Cambridge Checkpoint/IGCSE/A-Level expectations, identifying gaps in curriculum
Special Focus PLCs: Temporary PLCs formed around specific initiative (EAL strategies PLC, STEAM integration PLC, Assessment Design PLC)
Best for: Targeted improvement in specific area; typically meet for one term or semester
content.title: 7.7.1 PLC Structure

items: All teachers participate in at least one PLC
PLC size: Ideally 4-8 members for productive discussion (larger groups can be subdivided)
Membership stable for the year to build trust and continuity
Frequency: Determined by school leadership; typically bi-weekly or monthly
Duration: 60-90 minutes per meeting
Scheduled: During school day (e.g., common planning period, late-start days) or immediately after school
Non-negotiable: PLC time is protected; meetings not canceled for other activities
PLC Leader/Facilitator:
Typically HOD or experienced teacher
Leadership can rotate to build capacity
Role: Set agenda, keep discussion focused, ensure all voices heard, manage time, document decisions
Shared Responsibility: All members contribute; PLC is collaborative, not top-down

content.text: PLCs in Eduversal schools use the Professional Learning Community model developed by Richard DuFour, centered on four critical questions that drive continuous improvement: Question 1: What do we want students to learn?

items: Review learning standards (Cambridge curriculum objectives, Indonesian Capaian Pembelajaran)
Clarify essential knowledge and skills (what's most important vs. nice-to-know)
Develop common understanding of "proficiency" (what does mastery look like?)
content.title: 7.7.2 DuFour's Four Critical Questions

items: Grade 9 IGCSE Math PLC: "For quadratic equations unit, students must be able to solve by factoring, completing the square, and using quadratic formula. They must also interpret solutions in real-world contexts. Let's agree that 'proficient' means correctly solving at least 7 out of 10 problems and explaining method choice."

content.text: Question 2: How will we know if they've learned it?

items: Design common assessments (all teachers in PLC give the same or equivalent assessment)
Agree on rubrics, scoring criteria, and standards for success
Calibrate scoring (moderation): Score sample student work together to ensure consistency

Grade 9 Math PLC creates common end-of-unit test on quadratic equations. Teachers each bring 3 sample student solutions. PLC scores together, discusses borderline cases, agrees on exemplars for each performance level (Below Basic, Basic, Proficient, Advanced).

content.text: Question 3: What will we do if they haven't learned it?

items: Plan interventions and re-teaching strategies for students who don't master content
Differentiate based on student needs (small group instruction, tutoring, modified tasks, peer support)
Monitor effectiveness of interventions; adjust if not working

After common assessment, 12 students in PLC's classes scored Below Basic on quadratic equations. PLC plans: (1) Small group re-teaching session during lunch or after school focusing on factoring misconceptions, (2) Peer tutoring pairs strong students with struggling students for homework support, (3) Re-assess after 1 week with parallel test to check growth.

content.text: Question 4: What will we do if they already know it?

items: Plan extensions, enrichment, and acceleration for students who have already mastered content
Challenge advanced students with deeper applications, independent projects, leadership opportunities

For 6 students who scored Advanced on quadratics, PLC plans: Enrichment project researching real-world applications of quadratics (projectile motion, architecture); challenge to derive quadratic formula algebraically; opportunity to peer-tutor struggling students (reinforces their mastery through teaching).

content.text: Typical PLC Meeting Agenda (60-90 minutes): 1. Check-In (5 minutes)

items: Quick round: Each member shares one success or challenge since last meeting
Builds relationships and collective awareness of what's happening in classrooms
content.title: 7.7.3 PLC Meeting Protocol

items: Revisit PLC agreements (start/end on time, all participate, assume positive intent, focus on evidence not opinions, maintain confidentiality of student data)
Norms posted visibly or reviewed verbally

content.text: 3. Analyze Student Data (20-30 minutes) (Heart of PLC Work)

items: Review results from common assessment, formative assessment, or recent unit
Identify patterns:
Which students/groups mastered objectives? Which didn't?
What questions or concepts were most difficult? Most successful?
Are there trends by class, teacher, student subgroup (EAL, SEN, gender)?
Discuss Root Causes (not blame):
Why did students struggle with this concept? (Prerequisite skills missing, instruction unclear, assessment poorly designed, insufficient practice time, other factors)
Why did this strategy work well? (Can we replicate it?)
Use Protocols for Data Analysis:
Display student work samples (anonymized)
Use data charts (% of students proficient by class/teacher)
Focus on evidence, not assumptions: "I noticed students confused 'x' with variable vs. multiplication sign" (evidence) not "Students don't pay attention" (assumption)

content.text: 4. Plan Action (20-30 minutes) Based on data analysis, PLC makes decisions about next instructional steps:

items: Interventions for Struggling Students:
Who needs additional support? What specific skills do they need?
What intervention strategies will we use? When? Who will deliver?
How will we progress-monitor?
Adjustments to Upcoming Lessons:
Based on what worked/didn't work, what will we change in next unit's lessons?
Do we need more time on a concept? Different examples? More practice?
Instructional Strategies to Try:
Share successful strategies: "When I used a real-world example of loans for teaching quadratics, students were much more engaged. Here's the activity..."
Agree to try a strategy collectively and report back next meeting

content.text: 5. Set Goals and Next Steps (10 minutes)

items: Commit to Actions: Each teacher commits to trying specific strategy or intervention before next meeting
Plan Next Common Assessment: What will we assess next? When? Who will draft it?
Assign Roles for Next Meeting: Facilitator, note-taker, timekeeper (rotate responsibilities to build leadership capacity)
Set Agenda Topics: What do we need to discuss next time?
What did we accomplish today?
What questions remain?
How is our PLC functioning? (Are norms being followed? Do we need to adjust anything?)

content.text: PLC Norms (Agreements for How We Work Together): PLCs function best when members agree on norms for collaboration. Common norms:

items: Focus on Learning: Keep student learning at center of all discussions (not adult preferences or convenience)
Collaborate, Don't Compete: Share strategies openly; teaching is not a competition
Use Data and Evidence: Base decisions on student work, assessment results, observation evidence (not hunches or anecdotes alone)
Be Respectful: Critique ideas, not people; assume positive intent; value diverse perspectives
Take Action: PLCs lead to change in practice; discussions must result in concrete next steps
Maintain Confidentiality: Student data discussed in PLC stays in PLC; don't gossip about students or colleagues
Honor Time: Start and end on time; stay focused on agenda; sidebar conversations can happen after meeting
content.title: 7.7.4 PLC Norms and Evidence

items: Attend regularly and actively participate (PLC attendance mandatory unless excused)
Come prepared: Bring student work, data, materials needed for discussion
Implement agreed-upon strategies: Try what PLC decides to try; report back on results
Contribute ideas: Don't just listen; share your insights, questions, strategies
Be open to feedback and change: Willingness to examine and improve practice

content.text: Evidence of Effective PLCs (Artifacts for Accreditation and Quality Assurance): Documentation provides evidence that PLCs are functioning as intended:

items: PLC Meeting Agendas and Notes: Record of discussions, decisions, action items (one member designated as note-taker each meeting)
Common Assessments Developed by PLC: Tests, quizzes, rubrics, projects designed collaboratively
Student Work Samples Analyzed: Anonymized samples with PLC annotations showing analysis
Data Charts/Graphs: Visual representations of student performance data reviewed by PLC
Intervention Plans: Documented strategies for supporting struggling students, progress monitoring data
Evidence of Impact: Before-and-after data showing improvement after PLC-designed interventions
Example: "After PLC decided to add more visual supports for EAL students in Science, % of EAL students scoring Proficient increased from 45% to 68% on next assessment."

content.text: Storage: PLC documentation stored in shared folder accessible to school leadership and accreditation teams. Demonstrates collaborative culture and continuous improvement. 7.7.5 Peer Observation Protocol Purpose: Learn from colleagues' effective practices through structured observation. Process:

items: Pre-Observation Meeting (15 min):
Observer and observee agree on focus area (e.g., "questioning techniques")
Observee shares lesson plan and context
Observation (full lesson, 30-45 min):
Observer takes notes on agreed focus area
Non-evaluative lens (not scoring, just learning)
Post-Observation Debrief (20 min):
Observee reflects first: "How did it go?"
Observer shares "I noticed..." observations (not judgments)
Collaborative discussion: "What might we try in our own practice?"

content.text: Frequency: Minimum 2 peer observations per term (1 as observer, 1 as observee) Documentation: Brief reflection log (What did I learn? What will I try?) stored in teacher's PD portfolio.
Purpose: Leverage multiple teachers' expertise for enhanced student learning. Common Co-Teaching Models:

steps: Team Teaching: Both teachers lead instruction together (ideal for STEAM, cross-curricular units)
Station Teaching: Divide class into groups; each teacher leads a station
Parallel Teaching: Split class in half; both teach same content (reduces student-teacher ratio)
One Teach, One Support: One leads, other circulates for individual support (use sparingly)
content.title: 7.7.6 Co-Teaching Models and Collaboration

steps: Shared lesson planning time (minimum 30 min/week)
Clear role definitions for each lesson
Shared assessment and grading
Regular debrief: "What worked? What do we adjust?"
English \+ Indonesian language teacher co-teaching reading comprehension
Science \+ STEAM teacher co-teaching design thinking project
Classroom teacher \+ SEN teacher co-teaching differentiated math lesson

content.text: Purpose: Empower teachers to investigate and improve their own practice systematically. Action Research Cycle:

steps: Identify a Problem of Practice: "My Year 9 students struggle with persuasive writing."
Research & Plan: Review literature, design intervention (e.g., peer review protocol)
Implement: Try intervention for 4-6 weeks
Collect Data: Student work samples, surveys, assessment results
Analyze & Reflect: Did it work? Why/why not?
Share Findings: Present to PLC or at school PD day
content.title: 7.7.7 Action Research for Professional Growth

content.text: Support: Teachers conducting action research may present findings at Eduversal Annual PD Conference.
Beyond Surveys: Engaging Students in Teacher Growth

steps: Exit Tickets with Teaching Feedback: "One thing that helped me learn today:" \+ "One thing that confused me:"
Student Focus Groups: 4-6 students meet with teacher quarterly to discuss "What helps you learn best in this class?"
Student-Led Conferences: Students present their learning progress to parents (teacher as facilitator)
content.title: 7.7.8 Amplifying Student Voice

content.text: Integrate Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics across the curriculum to foster creativity, critical thinking, collaboration, and real-world problem-solving skills. STEAM education prepares students for 21st-century challenges and careers while aligning with Cambridge curriculum and Indonesian educational goals (Profil Pelajar Pancasila).
content.title: Purpose

content.text: STEAM Definition: STEAM is a learning approach that connects Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics to solve real-world problems in creative, hands-on ways. It breaks down traditional subject silos, allowing students to see interconnections between disciplines and apply knowledge authentically. Purpose of STEAM Education: 1. Critical Thinking and Problem-Solving STEAM encourages students to approach challenges methodically using evidence-based reasoning and experimentation. Through hands-on projects like designing a sustainable bridge or programming a robot, students learn to analyze data, test hypotheses, iterate on solutions, and build resilience—skills essential for navigating complex real-world issues. 2. Interdisciplinary Knowledge By blending multiple subjects, STEAM breaks down silos between disciplines. Students see how professionals in fields like architecture, game design, or environmental science apply math for calculations, art for aesthetics, science for understanding systems, and engineering for functionality. This leads to comprehensive understanding and innovative outcomes. 3. Creativity and Collaboration STEAM emphasizes artistic elements to spark imagination while group activities promote teamwork and communication. Students might collaborate on a multimedia project like creating an animated story about environmental science, which hones their ability to brainstorm, share feedback, refine work collectively—skills vital for modern workplaces. 4. 21st-Century Skills In an era driven by rapid technological advancement, STEAM equips learners with competencies like digital literacy, adaptability, and innovation. It transforms students from passive consumers of technology into active creators, readying them for careers in emerging fields such as AI, renewable energy, robotics, and creative industries. STEAM Across K-12: STEAM has a significant role across all grade levels, adapting to students' developmental stages:

items: Primary (Grades 1-6): Exploration and curiosity through hands-on activities (building simple machines, coding with Scratch Jr., art-science integration)
Lower Secondary (Grades 7-8): Foundational skills and creativity (LEGO robotics, basic coding, design thinking projects)
IGCSE (Grades 9-10): Applied STEAM and analytical skills (VEX robotics, web development, engineering design challenges, competition preparation)
A-Level (Grades 11-12): Advanced specialization and competition-level projects (Arduino robotics, machine learning, CAD design, research projects for international competitions)
content.title: 7.8.1 What is STEAM Education?

content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools implement STEAM through three complementary phases: Phase 1: Weekly Lessons (During Regular School Hours)

items: Integration: STEAM concepts embedded into regular subject lessons (Science, Math, IT, Art, Design & Technology classes)
Frequency: Ongoing throughout academic year
Examples:
Science: Conduct experiments using scientific method; collect and analyze data
Math: Solve real-world problems using mathematical modeling
Art: Create visual representations of scientific concepts or mathematical patterns
Technology: Use coding to simulate scientific phenomena
content.title: 7.8.2 STEAM Implementation Framework

content.text: Phase 2: After-School Programs (2-3 times per week, 1.5-2 hours)

items: Purpose: Extended time for hands-on projects, deeper exploration, skill development
Target: Students interested in intensive STEAM work beyond classroom
Activities: Robotics clubs, coding workshops, maker activities, competition teams
Duration: One academic term or year-long

content.text: Phase 3: Weekend Intensive Programs (6 weekends per term, 4 hours per session)

items: Purpose: Immersive STEAM experiences; focused skill-building; competition preparation
Structure: Saturday or Sunday sessions with specialized tracks
Target: Motivated students preparing for national/international STEAM competitions
Tracks (students select one):

steps: Interdisciplinary Projects
Robotics
Coding
Design & Modeling
Competition Preparation

content.text: Integration Strategies: Project-Based Learning (PBL) STEAM naturally integrates with PBL. Students work on extended, authentic projects that require applying multiple disciplines:

items: Example: Grade 8 "Sustainable City Design" project
Science: Research renewable energy sources, environmental impact
Math: Calculate costs, scale drawings
Technology: Use CAD software to design city infrastructure; create digital presentations
Engineering: Build physical model with sustainable features (solar panels, green spaces, water recycling)
Arts: Design aesthetically pleasing city layout; create marketing materials to "sell" the sustainable city concept
content.title: 7.8.3 Teaching STEAM Across the Curriculum

content.text: Real-World Problem-Solving Connect learning to issues students care about or see in their communities:

items: Example Topics: Plastic pollution in Indonesian waters, clean water access, renewable energy for rural communities, food waste reduction
STEAM Approach for "Clean Water" Project:
Science: Analyze water quality data; understand contamination sources
Math: Calculate filtration rates, cost-benefit analysis of solutions
Technology: Design apps to track water quality or educate community
Engineering: Build water filtration systems using local materials
Arts: Create awareness campaigns with posters, videos, infographics

content.text: Inquiry-Based Learning Start with compelling questions that require students to investigate and discover:

items: Essential Questions for STEAM:
How can we reduce our school's carbon footprint?
What makes a bridge strong and stable?
How can technology help solve food security challenges in Indonesia?
How does art communicate scientific concepts?

content.text: Design Thinking Process Teach students the iterative design process used by engineers and innovators:

steps: Empathize: Understand the problem and user needs
Define: Clearly articulate the problem to solve
Ideate: Brainstorm multiple possible solutions
Prototype: Build quick, testable models
Test: Gather feedback; identify what works and what doesn't
Iterate: Refine design based on testing; repeat cycle

content.text: Example: Grade 10 students use design thinking to create assistive devices for people with disabilities Subject-Specific STEAM Integration Examples: Science Classes

items: Incorporate engineering challenges (build a device to protect an egg dropped from 3 meters)
Use technology for data collection and visualization (sensors, Excel/Google Sheets, graphing software)
Connect to arts through scientific illustration, models, infographics
Apply math to real-world contexts (architecture, sports analytics, financial planning, coding)
Use technology for mathematical modeling and simulations
Explore mathematical patterns in nature and art (fractals, golden ratio, tessellations)
Research STEAM topics and communicate findings through writing
Analyze how STEM concepts are portrayed in literature and media
Create multimedia presentations combining text, visuals, and technology
Explore intersection of art and science (color theory and light, perspective and geometry)
Use technology for digital art creation (graphic design, animation, 3D modeling)
Design functional and beautiful objects (form follows function in industrial design)
Research Indonesian scientists, engineers, and innovators
Document traditional Indonesian technology and innovations (batik, shipbuilding, irrigation systems)
Connect Profil Pelajar Pancasila (P5) dimensions to STEAM values

content.text: Teacher Collaboration for STEAM Effective STEAM implementation thrives on interdisciplinary teamwork among educators. Schools facilitate this through: Joint Planning Sessions

items: Schedule regular co-planning time for teachers from different subjects
Example: Science, Math, Art, and Technology teachers plan integrated unit on "Sustainable Architecture"
Align on shared learning outcomes, assessment, and timeline
Team-teach STEAM lessons with expertise from multiple disciplines
Example: Math teacher and Art teacher co-teach lesson on geometric patterns in Islamic art and architecture
Use collaborative tools (Google Drive, shared planning documents) to exchange resources, lesson ideas, and student work examples
Design projects that explicitly require multiple subjects
Create shared rubrics assessing both content-specific and cross-disciplinary skills (collaboration, creativity, communication)

content.text: For After-School and Weekend STEAM Programs, students select specialized tracks aligned with their interests and competition goals: Track 1: Interdisciplinary Projects

items: Focus: Solve real-world problems integrating multiple STEAM disciplines
Activities:
Lower Secondary: Design sustainable mini-gardens; create earthquake-resistant structures
IGCSE: Develop water-saving irrigation systems; build renewable energy prototypes
A-Level: Design IoT-based smart home systems; create accessible technology for underserved communities
content.title: 7.8.4 STEAM Tracks (Robotics, Coding, Design, Projects)

items: Focus: Design, build, and program robots for specific challenges
Tools:
Lower Secondary: LEGO Mindstorms EV3
IGCSE: VEX Robotics kits
A-Level: Arduino, Raspberry Pi, custom-built robots
Activities:
Autonomous navigation challenges
Object manipulation tasks
Line-following and maze-solving robots
Simulated competition scenarios (WRO, FIRST Robotics)
Focus: Software development, app creation, algorithms, computational thinking
Languages/Tools:
Lower Secondary: Scratch, Blockly, Code.org
IGCSE: Python, HTML/CSS/JavaScript, App Lab
A-Level: Python (advanced), Java, machine learning libraries, web frameworks
Activities:
Create games and simulations
Build web applications and interactive websites
Develop mobile apps addressing real-world problems
Explore AI/machine learning projects (image recognition, data analysis)
Focus: 2D/3D design, CAD, product prototyping, architectural modeling
Tools:
Lower Secondary: Tinkercad, SketchUp
IGCSE: Fusion 360, Blender (basic), laser cutters, 3D printers
A-Level: AutoCAD, Blender (advanced), professional-grade CAD software
Activities:
Design functional products (phone stands, organizers, prosthetics)
Create architectural models (sustainable buildings, bridges)
Produce 3D-printed prototypes of inventions
Explore digital fabrication techniques
Focus: Intensive training for national and international STEAM competitions (see Section 7.8.5)
Structure: Mentorship from experienced competition coaches; practice with past competition challenges; mock judging and pitch practice
Activities: Work on competition-specific projects with guidance on documentation, presentation, and judging criteria
Interdisciplinary: Build mini wind turbine; test and optimize blade design
Robotics: Program LEGO robot to navigate obstacle course
Coding: Create animated story in Scratch about climate change
Design: Model sustainable playground using Tinkercad

Integrate technology effectively to enhance teaching and learning, prepare students for a digital world, and provide flexible, personalized learning experiences. Digital tools are pedagogical tools, not substitutes for quality teaching.
content.title: Purpose

content.text: The SAMR Model (Ruben Puentedura) provides a framework for evaluating technology use in lessons: Level 1: Substitution (Technology substitutes traditional tool with no functional change)

items: Example: Students type essay in Google Docs instead of handwriting
Still useful (easier editing, spell-check) but not transformative
content.title: 7.9.1 SAMR Model for Technology Integration

content.text: Level 2: Augmentation (Technology substitutes with functional improvement)

items: Example: Students collaborate in real-time on shared Google Doc; teacher provides comments; version history tracked
Enhancement: Collaboration, feedback, revision process improved
Example: Students create multimedia presentation combining text, images, embedded videos, hyperlinks to sources
Task redesigned: Couldn't easily do this without technology

content.text: Level 4: Redefinition (Technology enables new tasks, previously inconceivable)

items: Example: Students in Indonesia collaborate with students in Kenya via video conference to jointly design solutions to clean water challenges; share data in real-time; co-create digital documentary
Completely new learning experience enabled by technology

content.text: Guiding Principle: Aim for Modification and Redefinition when possible, but Substitution/Augmentation still have value when they make learning more efficient or accessible. Technology should serve clear learning objectives, not be used for technology's sake.

items: Use tools beyond static slides: Prezi, Canva, Nearpod (interactive slides with embedded questions), Pear Deck
Embed multimedia: videos, simulations, 3D models, audio clips
Make presentations interactive: polls, quizzes, discussion prompts embedded throughout
content.title: 7.9.2 Digital Learning Strategies

items: Kahoot, Quizizz, Blooket: Gamified quizzes for review and engagement
Google Forms: Exit tickets, self-assessments, surveys
Padlet: Digital bulletin board for brainstorming, sharing ideas, peer feedback
Mentimeter: Live polls, word clouds, Q\&A during lessons
Use data from digital assessments to adjust instruction in real-time
Google Workspace: Docs, Slides, Sheets for collaborative writing, presentations, data analysis
Jamboard: Digital whiteboard for brainstorming
Flip (formerly Flipgrid): Video discussion platform for asynchronous sharing and response
Teach digital collaboration norms: all contribute, respectful communication, proper attribution
PhET (University of Colorado): Free interactive STEM simulations
Labster: Virtual science lab experiences
GeoGebra: Math visualization and exploration
Use when physical labs unavailable, dangerous, or impractical; supplement, don't always replace, hands-on experiments
Students curate their best work digitally (Google Sites, Seesaw, portfolios in LMS)
Benefits: Reflection, growth over time visible, easily shared with parents/future teachers/universities
Include written reflections on learning process, not just finished products
Students watch instructional videos or complete readings at home
Class time devoted to practice, application, discussion, problem-solving with teacher support
Works well for older students (IGCSE, A-Level) with reliable internet access
Caution: Ensure equity (not all students have home internet); provide alternatives (watch videos during study hall, library access)

Posting responsibly
